barks.text: Okay. Grandpa's board. He'd sit here every night with his coffee going cold and say: a story you can't SEE isn't a story yet, it's a rumor with a byline.
barks.speaker: gary

barks.text: First rule: everything I learned today goes on the cork. Doesn't matter how small. Pin it. Let's start easy — the case of Dot's disappearing red pens. Pin what I know.
barks.speaker: gary

barks.text: Good. Now the other half. A fact alone is just lonely. Pin the notebook — the one labeled CRIMES, question mark, written entirely in urgent red ink.
barks.speaker: gary

barks.text: Here's the whole job, kid — Grandpa's words, not mine: when two things SUPPORT each other, you tie them together. Hold both cards, tie the string, and see if the story clicks.
barks.speaker: gary

barks.text: THERE. Hear that? That's the click. Missing pens plus a kid who writes in nothing but red — that's not two facts anymore, that's a STORY. Gold string means it holds.
barks.speaker: gary

barks.text: One more thing. Those index cards along the bottom — my open questions, in my own handwriting, with slots for what each answer still needs. When a question's slots fill up, tie its cards and the story clicks. That's the loop. Every night, same loop.
barks.speaker: gary

barks.text: Right. You know the drill. The cork remembers even when I don't.
barks.speaker: gary

barks.text: No. Hm. Maybe start with what I'm actually asking — the questions are right there at the bottom of the board.
barks.speaker: gary
